topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The pile of unopened mail on the kitchen counter seemed to mock me. I dragged myself to the chair, the movement feeling like wading through thick syrup. My eyes, heavy-lidded and unfocused, drifted over the glossy paper. Then I saw it: a perfectly formatted essay, titled "The Uncharted Territory." My breath hitched, a small, involuntary sound escaping my throat. It was my own story, meticulously polished, sitting right there in the junk mail. I picked it up, the paper feeling strangely light, and began to read. The words flowed, and for a moment, the inertia that had been clinging to my limbs seemed to lift, replaced by a strange, distant sense of accomplishment.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the blue light burning against my tired eyes. Every muscle in my neck felt weighted down, a dull ache radiating from my shoulders. I had been procrastinating all afternoon, just letting the minutes dissolve into one another. Then, a notification popped up. It was an email, an attachment labeled "Sample Application Essay." My heart gave a slow, heavy thud against my ribs. Clicking the file, I found it—my words, transformed, presented as an exemplar. A slow, creeping realization washed over me, heavy and unwelcome. I leaned back in my worn office chair, the springs groaning in protest, and just let my gaze trace the carefully chosen sentences.

[story 3]

The afternoon sun slanted weakly through the dusty window, illuminating the motes dancing in the stagnant air. I hadn't moved much since lunch; the world felt muffled, distant. While shuffling through a stack of forgotten textbooks, my fingers brushed against a crisp, cream-colored sheet. It was an essay. My stomach tightened with a strange mixture of dread and recognition. Opening it felt like lifting a stone. The prose was sharp, vibrant—a version of my own narrative, perfectly articulated. I reread the opening paragraph, and a profound sense of inertia settled back over me, heavier this time, as if the sheer weight of the perfect example had pressed me back into stillness.
